summary_preview:
音乐行业高管往往不愿与粉丝直接打交道，因为粉丝群体复杂多变——喜好难测，社群多元，受其他粉丝意见影响深远。尽管可以通过刷榜、买流媒体、囤积唱片或雇人到场等方式制造虚假热度，但真正有价值的艺术作品，终究需要真实、持续且发自内心的支持。

然而，一些公司如Chaotic Good正试图“制造”粉丝，将粉丝社群包装成可操控的资源。他们通过策划“叙事战役”等手段，系统性地引导舆论、操控话题，甚至让艺术家与粉丝之间的连接变得可计算、可量产。令人担忧的是，这些做法模糊了真实喜爱与人为操控的界限。

值得注意的是，该文章发布后不久，Chaotic Good便大幅修改其官网，移除了“叙事战役”相关内容，部分合作艺人也从其页面消失。尽管如此，这些行为仍值得警惕——当粉丝的热情被精心设计与利用，艺术本应承载的情感连接，也可能沦为一场精心策划的商业表演。

***Edit 4/2:** One day after this piece went up, Chaotic Good made significant changes to their website — including pulling the “Narrative Campaign” section completely. Some of the artists I write about here no longer feature on the website, though it is not clear if they are still clients of Chaotic Good (my suspicion is that they are, and that their managements are removing public associations with the company). That being said, some of the examples I cited here can no longer be traced back to their website, but feel free to use the waybackmachine or similar to check my work!*

--- 元数据 ---
标题：假粉丝
作者：eliza mclamb
网站：无
最终URL：https://www.wordsfromeliza.com/p/fake-fans
提取方式：readability
--- Markdown ---
***编辑 4/2：** 这篇文章发布一天后，Chaotic Good 对其网站进行了重大更改——包括完全移除了“叙事活动”部分。我在此文中提到的一些艺术家已不再出现在该网站上，但尚不清楚他们是否仍是 Chaotic Good 的客户（我推测他们仍然是，而他们的管理团队正在主动移除公开关联）。尽管如此，我文中引用的一些案例已无法在原网站上追溯，但欢迎使用互联网档案馆（waybackmachine）等工具来验证我的内容！*

If they had it their way, music business executives would rather not deal with the fans at all.

如果由他们决定，音乐行业高管们更愿意完全不与粉丝打交道。

Fans are a complicated, messy, unpredictable group. Sometimes they love a record, sometimes they hate it. Sometimes they love the single and hate the record. Sometimes they love the record and hate the single. They’re teenage girls, oldhead uncles, Gwenyth Paltrow, and the guy checking your groceries out at the Safeway. Their communities are niche and complex, their tastes formed not only by the artists they listen to but by the opinions of other fans. As an executive, you can inflate the charts, you can buy streams. You can buy vinyl (though who even cares about that). You can even pay people to crowd the shows once or twice. But for any art worth your investment, you will eventually need real people to reliably, measureably, and genuinely care about it.

粉丝是一群复杂、混乱且不可预测的人。他们有时喜爱一张专辑，有时又讨厌它；有时喜欢单曲却讨厌整张专辑，有时又喜欢专辑却讨厌单曲。他们可能是十几岁的少女，也可能是年长的叔叔，是格温妮丝·帕特洛，或是你在Safeway结账时遇到的那个家伙。他们的社群往往小众而复杂，他们的品味不仅受所听艺术家影响，也受其他粉丝意见的塑造。作为高管，你可以操控排行榜，可以购买播放量，可以购买黑胶唱片（尽管如今谁还关心这个呢）。你甚至可以花钱雇人去现场凑热闹一两次。但对于任何值得你投入的作品，最终你仍需要真实的人，持续、可量化、且发自内心地热爱它。

In the dream world of an executive, fandom is something like a parasitic disease — contagious through mere exposure, trafficking quickly between hosts with immediate contact and little to no external intervention. This way, the executive needs only to create a Patient Zero and watch the snowball gather material. The disease would spread seamlessly, easily. It would be viral.

在高管的理想世界里，粉丝文化就像一种寄生性疾病——只需接触即可传播，迅速在用户之间传染，几乎无需外部干预。这样一来，高管只需创造一个‘零号病人’，然后看着这场雪球效应不断积累。这种传播将顺滑而轻松，近乎病毒式扩散。

Last week, I came across [a Billboard interview](https://www.billboard.com/video/secrets-to-song-virality-on-tiktok-w-digital-marketing/) with the founders of Chaotic Good Projects, a digital marketing agency that promises to create virality by, among other things, manufacturing hundreds of fake fan accounts for musicians.

上周，我偶然读到了一篇[《公告牌》的专访](https://www.billboard.com/video/secrets-to-song-virality-on-tiktok-w-digital-marketing/)，采访对象是数字营销公司 Chaotic Good Projects 的创始人。该公司承诺，通过制造数百个虚假粉丝账号，帮助音乐人实现病毒式传播。

Having been a working musician for the better part of the last decade, this was not particularly surprising to me. Commercial music exists for a reason (it is widely liked and extremely profitable), and it is no secret that there is a gigantic machine that is not only behind our biggest stars, but playing a part in breaking the new mainstream. There are *kinds* of music that are compatible with TikTok trends, and others that rely on a broader context, a less immediate delivery. Distinguished taste is something people pride themselves on — the idea that they have some resistance left still, that they don’t have to listen to the Alex Warrens of the Sombrs of the world simply because the algorithm has offered its teat to suck.

作为一名在音乐行业打拼近十年的音乐人，这对我来说并不感到意外。商业音乐的存在自有其原因（它广受欢迎且利润丰厚），也毫无秘密可言：在我们最顶级的明星背后，有一个庞大的机器，正在推动新主流的崛起。有些类型的音乐与TikTok趋势兼容，而另一些则依赖更广泛的文化语境和更非即时的表达方式。人们以拥有‘独特品味’而自豪——认为自己仍保有某种抵抗能力，不会仅仅因为算法推了，就去听亚历克斯·沃伦或索姆布之类的歌手的作品。

Alex Warren and Sombr are, to no one’s surprise, clients of Chaotic Good Projects. These two are part of the new mainstream broken primarily by algorithmic social media platforms, though Chaotic Good’s client list also includes more established pop giants like Dua Lipa, Shawn Mendes, and Justin Bieber. The careers of these people, while obviously influential in my industry, are not really what I’m interested in. I’m interested in the people who made it without capitulating completely to commercial demands, whose personas don’t eclipse their work. People who are making stuff that’s simultaneously viable enough to be profitable and still uncompromising on a vision. I’m interested in the real rock stars, if we have any left.

亚历克斯·沃伦和索姆布，毫不意外，都是 Chaotic Good Projects 的客户。这两人是算法社交媒体平台主导下新主流的代表人物，不过 Chaotic Good 的客户名单还涵盖更多成熟流行巨星，如杜阿·利帕、肖恩·门德斯和贾斯汀·比伯。这些人的职业生涯，尽管显然对我所在的行业具有影响力，却并非我真正关注的对象。我更感兴趣的是那些并未完全屈从于商业需求、个人形象不压倒作品的音乐人。他们创作的作品既具备商业可行性，又能坚持个人艺术主张。我关心的是真正的摇滚明星——如果如今还有的话。

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yOGu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb1d34460-fe78-42fd-9b08-ca235e413f2b_620x422.webp)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yOGu!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb1d34460-fe78-42fd-9b08-ca235e413f2b_620x422.webp)

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yOGu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb1d34460-fe78-42fd-9b08-ca235e413f2b_620x422.webp)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yOGu!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb1d34460-fe78-42fd-9b08-ca235e413f2b_620x422.webp)

playing with the piano facing away from the audience is verifiable rockstar shit

背对观众弹钢琴，这简直是可验证的摇滚巨星行为

The first time I heard Cameron Winter’s “Love Takes Miles,” I probably heard it one hundred times in a row. I had found it a week after its release and became immediately convinced that I was one of the few people in the world who knew about this perfect, beautiful little secret. At the time, the song had just under a million streams, and I was obsessed with showing it to everyone. Everyone soon caught on. The next year, it was the summer of “Love Takes Miles.” I played it in rental cars in Los Angeles and off my phone speakers in the most remote parts of Chimney Rock, North Carolina. The rest of that record was a similar revelation for me. I had discovered some kind of magic.

我第一次听到卡梅隆·温特的《Love Takes Miles》时，可能连着听了一百遍。我在专辑发布一周后发现了这首歌，并立刻确信自己是世界上少数几个知道这个完美而美妙小秘密的人之一。当时这首歌的流媒体播放量刚过百万，我热衷于向所有人推荐。很快，大家都注意到了。第二年夏天，成了《Love Takes Miles》的夏天。我在洛杉矶租来的车里播放它，在北卡罗来纳州奇尼岩最偏远的角落，用手机音箱放着它。这张专辑的其余部分也让我产生了类似的震撼。我仿佛发现了一种魔法。

Though, I can’t remember exactly how I discovered it. I didn’t hear about the song from a friend or a music blog, and can’t recall a particular memory — only that of seeing the title somewhere on my phone and searching it up on Spotify. How I came to know the song is almost irrelevant information at this point, eclipsed completely by the experience of loving the song on my own terms, creating my own memories with it. The song just came to me, from somewhere, populating seamlessly in a stream of consciousness. A stream of consciousness, otherwise known as an algorithm.

不过，我已经记不清自己究竟是怎么发现这首歌的。我并非从朋友或音乐博客那里听说的，也想不起某个具体的记忆——只记得在手机某个地方看到过歌名，便在Spotify上搜索并找到了它。如今，我如何得知这首歌已变得无关紧要，完全被自己独享这首歌的体验、为它创造的回忆所掩盖。这首歌就这样悄然出现，从某处涌入我的意识流中——而这种意识流，正是算法的体现。

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yKaD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9401faa1-c4d7-40f0-96af-31947cb5c174_1170x717.jpeg)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yKaD!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9401faa1-c4d7-40f0-96af-31947cb5c174_1170x717.jpeg)

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yKaD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9401faa1-c4d7-40f0-96af-31947cb5c174_1170x717.jpeg)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yKaD!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9401faa1-c4d7-40f0-96af-31947cb5c174_1170x717.jpeg)

“Loves Takes Miles” was worked as a part of a “narrative campaign” by Chaotic Good Projects. A “narrative campaign” is one of four kinds of services the agency offers — the others being “UGC” (which stands for user-generated content), “Fanpage,” and “Brands and Media.” According to the agency’s website, they also worked narrative and UGC campaigns for Geese’s record “Getting Killed.”

《Love Takes Miles》是 Chaotic Good Projects 所谓“叙事活动”的一部分。所谓“叙事活动”，是该机构提供的四种服务之一，其他包括“UGC”（用户生成内容）、“粉丝页面”和“品牌与媒体合作”。根据该机构官网信息，他们也为乐队 Geese 的专辑《Getting Killed》策划了叙事与UGC活动。

When I discovered that the same media apparatus propping up Sombr and Alex Warren (the term propping up here is used intentionally — to me, there is almost nothing compelling about these artists aside from their aggressive social campaign) was also boosting Cameron Winter and Geese, I was shocked. I thought this was the kind of thing that was only deployed in service of mass-market, commercial pop — secretly, also, that this was the only kind of music such marketing would *work* for.

当我发现，那个支撑着索姆布和亚历克斯·沃伦的媒体机器（此处使用‘支撑’一词是刻意的——在我看来，除了其狂热的社交媒体攻势，这些艺术家本身几乎没什么令人信服之处）竟然也在助推卡梅隆·温特和 Geese 时，我感到震惊。我以为这类操作只用于主流商业流行音乐，也私下觉得，只有这类音乐才可能被这种营销方式“奏效”。

Alternative music used to mean just that — an alternative to the mainstream — something that couldn’t simply be adopted by everyone else through pure exposure, through virality. There are certainly arguments to be made about the mass appeal of a band like Geese, but no one in good faith could compare them to the commercial pop stars that populate Chaotic Good’s roster.

曾经，‘另类音乐’意味着真正的另类——一种无法通过简单曝光或病毒式传播被大众轻易接纳的东西。当然，关于 Geese 乐队的广泛吸引力，也存在一些讨论，但没有谁会诚实地将他们与 Chaotic Good 客户名单中的商业流行巨星相提并论。

But the roster runs deep, far past the predictable internet sensations one could expect (and, apologies to the internet sensations, including people I consider to be genuinely great musicians). Geese and Cameron Winter, but also Dijon and Mk.gee. Laufey and Wet Leg. Oklou and Jane Remover.

但该名单远不止人们预料中的那些网络红人（对这些网络红人，包括一些我认为确实是优秀音乐人的朋友，我在此致歉）。除了 Geese 和卡梅隆·温特，还包括迪荣（Dijon）和 Mk.gee。劳菲（Laufey）和 Wet Leg。奥克卢（Oklou）和简·雷莫弗（Jane Remover）。

I have no doubt that these artists could have ascended without the assistance of Chaotic Good because they are great musicians, and many people enjoy their music. I believe that this could have happened in the same way that I believe my friends in bands who play at bars could be the biggest musicians in the world if the industry were willing to facilitate their exposure. Much is said about hundreds of thousands of AI songs being uploaded to streaming services every day. Not enough is said about the many bands that are playing to no one, so many bands that could be huge if given the opportunity to be heard.

我毫不怀疑这些音乐人即使没有 Chaotic Good 的帮助，也能取得成功，因为他们本身就是出色的音乐人，且许多人喜欢他们的音乐。我之所以相信这一点，就像我相信我那些在酒吧演出的朋友，如果行业愿意提供曝光机会，他们也能成为世界顶级音乐人。我们常听到每天有数十万首AI生成的歌曲被上传到流媒体平台。但很少有人讨论那些在无人倾听的角落演奏的乐队——有多少乐队具备成为巨星的潜力，只因缺乏被听见的机会。

But the industry has changed. “Being heard” is not just about putting out music or even promoting it. The gatekeepers hardly matter anymore. SNL performances and favorable Pitchfork reviews don’t move the needle — TikToks *about* those things do (Chaotic Good ran a UGC campaign specifically to promote Oklou’s Tiny Desk performance). So, in this new landscape, is creating hundreds of fake accounts just par for the course of being a good publicist?

但行业已经变了。‘被听见’不再只是发布音乐或宣传而已。守门人已不再重要。SNL演出或Pitchfork的好评，已无法真正推动销量——真正起作用的是关于这些事件的TikTok视频（Chaotic Good曾专门策划UGC活动，推广Oklou的Tiny Desk演出）。因此，在这个新环境中，创建数百个虚假账号，是否已成为优秀公关的常态？

Though their website gives next-to-no details aside from who their clients are, Chaotic Good’s UGC (user-generated content) campaigns seem self-explanatory. Chaotic Good is paid to create accounts that generate content and simulate trends, which will ideally result in organic users generating content to further the trends themselves. Founders Jesse Coren and Andrew Spelman know that the internet is highly suggestible, which also happens to be the ethos of their narrative campaigns.

尽管其官网除客户名单外几乎未提供任何细节，但 Chaotic Good 的UGC（用户生成内容）活动看似一目了然。该公司受雇创建账号，生成内容并模拟趋势，理想情况下，这些行为将促使真实用户自发参与，进一步推动趋势。创始人杰西·科恩与安德鲁·斯佩尔曼深知，互联网用户极易被引导，而这正是他们叙事活动的哲学核心。

“A lot of what we do on the narrative side will be controlling the discourse,” said the founders, “I think most people see a video or see something about an album that came out and it’s like the first thing that they see or that first comment that they see is their opinion even when they haven’t heard the whole album.”

“我们在叙事层面做的很多工作，都是在控制舆论。”创始人表示，“我认为大多数人看到一段视频，或看到某张专辑发布的消息时，第一眼看到的评论，或第一个评论，就成了他们的观点，即便他们还没听完整张专辑。”

Well, that sounds quite unfair. What is an agency to do when its clients are at the mercy of an unruly TikTok comment section? They become the comment section.

这听起来非常不公平。当客户受制于不可控的TikTok评论区时，一个公司还能做什么？他们只能成为评论区本身。

“I think in the past, let’s say like a label and a management team do a great job. They get their artists on SNL or Tiny Desk or Triple J or something like that. Then they post it and then they kind of wait for the comments \[…\] what we do at Chaotic and with our management clients is, the second SNL drops at midnight, you should post a hundred times saying that was the best performance of the year.”

“过去，比如唱片公司或经纪团队做得很好，让艺人登上SNL或Tiny Desk或Triple J之类的节目。然后他们上传视频，接着就等着评论。而我们 Chaotic Good 及我们的经纪客户会做的是：SNL节目一在午夜上线，你就要立刻发布一百条内容，宣称这是‘年度最佳演出’。”

The phrase “you should post a hundred times” is said again and again over the course of the Billboard interview. As an artist, I am used to getting that instruction myself from management and label executives — post, post, post, try to hit the algorithm, fail miserably, post, post, post again, try covering other songs, try posting memes, try posting your feet, try posting your songs again, well, no, try posting the feet again. Coren and Spelman know that sucks, and their solution is for the burden to be shifted away from artists and onto agencies like theirs. When they say, “you should post a hundred times,” it is an instruction for their own employees and contractors and, for once, not the artists themselves.

在《公告牌》的采访中，“你应该发一百次”这句话被反复提及。作为一名艺术家，我早已习惯从管理层和唱片公司高管那里听到这种指令——发帖，发帖，发帖，试图迎合算法，惨败而归，再发帖，再发帖，尝试翻唱其他歌曲，尝试发段子，尝试发自己的脚，再尝试发自己的歌，嗯，不对，再发脚。科伦和斯佩尔曼知道这很糟糕，他们的解决方案是将这种负担从艺术家身上转移，转嫁给他们这样的代理机构。当他们说‘你应该发一百次’时，这其实是对他们自己员工和合同工的指令，而不再是针对艺术家本人了。

To be honest, it sounds appealing to me. I’ve written extensively about [the burden of the attention economy](https://www.wordsfromeliza.com/p/the-algorithm-killed-the-radio-star) [on artists](https://www.wordsfromeliza.com/p/direct-address) and how it [denigrates artistic efforts](https://www.wordsfromeliza.com/p/the-right-to-art-fandom-parasociality) as a whole, especially as it applies to the relationship fans have to the work. I have no doubt that musicians would literally pay to not have to do this, which is exactly how Chaotic Good makes their money. The founders don’t shy away from the dystopian implications of the new algorithmic economy, either; “it does get overwhelming because I don’t think the pendulum is swinging back anytime soon. I think what we’re seeing is things get more and more extreme.”

说实话，这听起来对我很有吸引力。我曾大量撰文探讨过注意力经济对艺术家的沉重负担[1]，以及它如何整体贬低艺术创作，尤其体现在粉丝与作品之间的关系上。我毫不怀疑，音乐人愿意付钱来摆脱这种任务——而这正是‘混乱良善’（Chaotic Good）的盈利模式。创始人也毫不回避这种新算法经济所蕴含的反乌托邦意味：‘这确实会令人不堪重负，因为我不认为天平会很快回调。我认为我们看到的，是情况变得越来越极端。’

The offer is not to create a new paradigm but to be the biggest fish in the pond. If 100 people think your song sucks, Chaotic Good will create 200 people who think your song is awesome. There’s no going back to pre-Napster times. Now that songs are files and files are Godless, record sales mean nothing, and the “analog revolution” won’t change that. Records used to mean fans, which meant money; now that there are no records, it’s just the fans and the money, and fans are a renewable resource. Chaotic Good knows that you can create a fan through imitation.

他们提供的并非创造新范式，而是成为池塘里最大的鱼。如果有100人认为你的歌难听，‘混乱良善’就能制造出200个认为你歌很棒的人。回到奈普斯特（Napster）之前的时代已不可能。如今歌曲就是文件，文件就是无神的，唱片销量已无意义，‘模拟革命’也无法改变这一点。过去唱片意味着粉丝，意味着金钱；现在没有了唱片，只剩下粉丝和金钱，而粉丝是一种可再生资源。‘混乱良善’明白，你可以通过模仿来制造粉丝。

When Coren and Spelman get specific about their marketing campaigns, they say that their team is constantly monitoring TikTok for trends. Quotes do particularly well, with the marketed song integrated seamlessly into the background. “Yeah, just kind of any quotes, just phrases that could be relevant to something that’s happening at the moment \[…\] It’s things you share with your boyfriend, your girlfriend, your brother, your sister.” The interviewer offers, “I’ve seen many times on my TikTok feed, maybe a picture of a coffee cup with a quote that’s like, “*I can’t believe I found you in this world.*””

当科伦和斯佩尔曼具体谈到他们的营销活动时，他们表示团队一直在持续监测TikTok上的趋势。引用类内容尤其有效，所推广的歌曲会无缝融入背景中。‘是的，只要是任何能与当下发生的事情产生关联的语句……比如你和男朋友、女朋友、兄弟、姐妹聊天时会用到的那些话。’采访者补充道：‘我在TikTok上看到过很多次，比如一张咖啡杯的照片配上一句类似‘我无法相信我在这个世界上找到了你’的文案。’

These are fans created through pure association — this quote reminded me of this person, and it just so happens that a song was attached to this experience. I may, then, listen to the song on my own in order to remind me of the experience I had of watching a TikTok. Chaotic Good seems to aim for such results.

这些粉丝是通过纯粹的关联制造出来的——这句话让我想起某个人，而恰好那首歌与这段经历相关联。于是，我可能会出于回忆观看TikTok的经历，而主动去听这首歌曲。‘混乱良善’显然正是追求这样的效果。

In order to boost the profile of indie folk musician Kevin Atwater (whose song “startripping” I found on TikTok years ago and played every day for months), Chaotic Good started pumping out edits to the show *Yellowjackets,* to much success: “The song has 40,000 creates on TikTok, and it’s really improved a lot \[…\] But what it’s done is now every time he posts with the audio, he’s supposed to do well because he has this trending audio, and it’s tied to his brand. And now fans show up dressed as the characters, and it’s kind of been this huge moment for him.”

为了提升独立民谣音乐人凯文·阿特沃特（Kevin Atwater）的知名度（他的歌曲《startripping》多年前我是在TikTok上发现的，我每天循环播放了数月），‘混乱良善’开始大量制作《黄蜂》（Yellowjackets）剧集的剪辑视频，取得了巨大成功：‘这首歌在TikTok上已有4万次创作使用，表现显著提升……但更重要的是，他每次发布带有该音频的内容时，都会获得良好表现，因为这个音频正在走红，这已经和他个人品牌绑定在一起。现在粉丝们甚至会装扮成剧中角色，对他而言，这已成为一个重要转折点。’

Is this success? *Yellowjackets* is a good show, which is lucky. But algorithmic associations are not always so tasteful. What if the nearest analog to “yearning” becomes the inevitable Ryan Murphy yaoi adaptation of the Columbine shooters? Those edits need soundtracks too — could it be your song?

这算是成功吗？《黄蜂》确实是一部好剧，这算是幸运。但算法关联并不总是那么有品位。假如‘渴望’最接近的类比，竟成了瑞安·墨菲（Ryan Murphy）对科伦拜恩枪击案的同性恋情改编？那些剪辑视频也需要配乐——会不会就是你的歌？

I’d let it be mine. My utopia knows nothing of “short form content” or “trend simulation” or “narrative campaigns,” but my utopia is a bedtime story I stopped telling myself the first time I got a $30 Spotify payout. This industry is a dirty one, and the cost of success has almost always been paid in dollars and cents — usually by a big label, a management company, or someone’s dad. If a label wanted to contract Chaotic Good on my behalf, I would accept such a deal handily and gratefully. The promise that industry has always made to artists is a tempting, paternal one: you create the magic, and I’ll take care of the rest.

我愿意让它成为我的歌。我的乌托邦里没有‘短内容’、没有‘趋势模拟’、没有‘叙事营销’，但我的乌托邦只是一个我早已在第一次收到30美元Spotify分成时就停止讲述的睡前故事。这个行业肮脏不堪，成功的代价几乎总是以金钱衡量——通常由大唱片公司、管理公司或某人的父亲支付。如果一家唱片公司想代表我与‘混乱良善’签约，我会欣然接受，并感激不尽。产业对艺术家所许诺的，始终是一种诱人的、父权式的承诺：你创造魔法，我来处理其余一切。

This isn’t to say that swimming against the current is futile, only that I see the kinds of developments made by Chaotic Good as an appropriate weapon in the algorithmic war of attrition. And indeed, Chaotic Good views this as a war; throughout the Billboard interview, the founders use terms like “hand-to-hand combat” to describe the process and referred to the early stages of production as “building an army.”

这并非意味着逆流而上毫无意义，只是我认为‘混乱良善’所推动的发展，是算法消耗战中一种恰当的武器。事实上，‘混乱良善’确实视其为一场战争；在整个《公告牌》采访中，创始人使用了诸如‘近身搏斗’这样的词汇来描述这一过程，并将早期制作阶段称为‘组建一支军队’。

I suspect that the more ubiquitous this service and its volumetric output become, the more bands will resist it, pulling back from streaming and socials altogether in favor of embracing hyper-local, scene-based methods of growth. [This wave has already started](https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-features/artists-left-spotify-ceo-daniel-ek-military-tech-1235425098/), with the rightful backlash to and divestment from streamers. I wonder about the longevity of the “trend simulation” strategy — if fans will eventually catch on to it, and thus eventually rebuke it. After all, nobody likes falling for marketing. It surprised me greatly to hear the founders of Chaotic Good talk so freely about who and how they manufacture hype for, as it seems to me that it would be in the interest of artists to withhold such information from the public. But perhaps there’s nothing to be ashamed about. Or, there’s no need to be more ashamed of the tactics created to get through the world than of the world itself

我推测，随着这项服务及其海量输出日益普及，越来越多乐队将开始抵制，转而完全退出流媒体与社交媒体，转而拥抱更加本地化、基于场景的传播方式。这种趋势其实早已开始[1]，人们对流媒体平台的反弹与撤资正日益明显。我好奇‘趋势模拟’策略的持久性——粉丝们最终是否会识破它，并因此拒绝它。毕竟，没人喜欢被营销耍了。令我惊讶的是，‘混乱良善’的创始人竟如此坦率地谈论他们如何制造热度、针对谁。在我看来，艺术家本应隐瞒这些信息才对。但或许，其实没有什么好羞愧的。又或者，我们不必为那些用于应对这个世界的策略感到比这个世界本身更羞耻。

（[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xY83!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5bc25fca-680f-439c-a747-f3e36551d030_745x525.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xY83!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5bc25fca-680f-439c-a747-f3e36551d030_745x525.png)）

one TikTok of this image with my song in the background would do my career more good than my favorable Pitchfork review

只要有一条TikTok视频配上我的歌，就能让我的职业生涯获益，远胜于一篇正面的《PITCHFORK》乐评

I wonder also about fandom, that complex web of mostly young women creating worlds for themselves and their friends online, using art as a compass for self-discovery, connection, and meaning. I wonder if the marketing accounts, who exist primarily to post, ever engage meaningfully with other, genuine fan accounts. It seems to their advantage to connect themselves to the network of real fans. Do they? Somewhere, is there a 27-year-old in a New York office reading Cameron Winter fanfic on AO3 in order to ingratiate himself into the larger, realer army of young fans who do their job for free?

我也在思考粉丝文化——那些由大多为年轻女性构建的复杂网络，她们在线上用艺术作为指南，探索自我、建立连接并赋予意义。我想知道，那些主要为发帖而存在的营销账号，是否真正与那些真实粉丝的账号有过有意义的互动。他们似乎有动机与真实粉丝网络建立联系。他们会吗？在某个地方，是否有一个27岁的纽约办公室职员，在AO3上阅读卡梅伦·温特（Cameron Winter）的同人小说，只为能融入那个真正为免费工作而存在的年轻粉丝大军？

Some of these processes can be automated, and I expect they will be soon; posting indiscriminately is the number one service of bots worldwide, and has been even before the technology became sophisticated. But other things, I have to believe, can’t be done by machines or even by real people who are invested in art primarily because that investment is made with real money. Some things can only be created by the fans themselves, without whom Chaotic Good and artists everywhere would never again cash a check.

其中一些流程可以自动化，我预计这很快就会实现；无差别发帖正是全球机器人最主要的服务，甚至在技术尚未成熟之前就是如此。但另一些事，我相信机器或哪怕只是投入了真金白银的艺术家本人也无法完成。有些东西只能由粉丝自己创造，没有他们，‘混乱良善’和所有艺术家都将永远无法兑现任何一张支票。

I’m on tour right now, trying my best to be present. I am promoting the shows, I am making short-form content, I am balancing the budget, I am trying desperately to find vegan options in North Dakota. Being on the road is kind of like being in a boat that’s full of holes, but you happen to have a bucket that’s exactly large enough to bail you out — as long as you keep hauling water. Debt can’t hit a target that’s always moving to the next city, trying to sell one more t-shirt. But when I have space to settle, I do. I look into the faces of the people in my crowd and become overwhelmed with love and gratitude, completely flooded with the kind of emotion I can only access in shared space, in shared work.

我目前正在进行巡演，努力让自己保持专注。我在推广演出，制作短内容，平衡预算，拼命想在北达科他州找到素食选项。巡演就像在一艘满是漏洞的船上，但恰好你手边有只刚好能舀出水的桶——只要不断舀水，就能撑住。债务无法击中一个永远朝着下一个城市移动、试图再卖一件T恤的人。但当我有片刻喘息时，我会停下。我凝视着观众的脸，爱与感激如潮水般涌来，完全被一种只能在共享空间、共享劳动中才可触及的情感淹没。

My record came out last October. It did not perform well, technically speaking. It’s not hitting large streaming numbers, none of my shows sold out, and I’m all but waiting to see if this is the year it’ll start putting me in serious debt. But I don’t know what hundreds of millions of streams would mean to me, what it would feel like. How do I touch that? How do I grasp it?

我的专辑去年十月发行。从技术角度看，它表现平平。它没有达到高流媒体数据，我的任何一场演出都没能售罄，我几乎只能等待，看这是否会成为让我陷入严重债务的一年。但我不清楚，几亿次流媒体播放对我意味着什么，那会是什么感觉。我该如何触及它？我该如何抓住它？

When I’m on the road, the thought strikes me hundreds of times a day: this is real. I see a wild buffalo rub its face against the mountainside. This is real. A teenager in the front row cries to a song I wrote when I was her age. This is real. The cliffside in rural Washington takes the breath from my lungs. This is real. I listen to “Love Takes Miles” with the windows down and think about my best friend, who I moved across the country to be near. This is real. My hands are on the fretboard. This is real. I run through the Badlands for the first time in my life, past the van, into the expanse. This is real. A sound escapes me and reaches you. This is real. This is real. This is real.

巡演途中，我每天会反复想到：这很真实。我看见一头野牛用脸蹭着山壁。这很真实。前排一位十几岁的女孩在听我写的歌时哭了——那首歌写于我跟她同龄时。这很真实。华盛顿乡间悬崖让我的呼吸停滞。这很真实。我开着窗听《Love Takes Miles》，想起我为了靠近最好的朋友而跨州搬迁。这很真实。我的手正放在琴弦上。这很真实。我第一次穿越荒原，越过小货车，进入无垠旷野。这很真实。一声低语从我喉咙溢出，传达到你耳中。这很真实。这很真实。这很真实。

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!oqcZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4908183b-bb75-4c85-ac2e-039fb977b605_3024x2185.heic)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!oqcZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4908183b-bb75-4c85-ac2e-039fb977b605_3024x2185.heic)

（[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!oqcZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4908183b-bb75-4c85-ac2e-039fb977b605_3024x2185.heic)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!oqcZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4908183b-bb75-4c85-ac2e-039fb977b605_3024x2185.heic)）

this is real

这很真实

Oh, yeah… [COME SEE ME ON TOUR!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!](https://www.bandsintown.com/a/15489846-eliza-mclamb?came_from=257&utm_medium=web&utm_source=home&utm_campaign=search_bar) thanks

title: Age verification on Systemd and Flatpak
fulltext:
--- METADATA ---
Title: Age verification on Systemd and Flatpak
Author: N/A
Site: Cybrkyd
FinalURL: https://cybrkyd.com/post/age-verification-on-systemd-and-flatpak/
Extraction: readability
--- MARKDOWN ---
* * *

* * *

Apple is rolling out [age verification for iPhone and iPad in the UK](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c20qwz9xzr9o). The idea is to get the device “owner” to verify their age so that they can access “certain services”. Apple has already removed encryption for UK users in 2025: [Apple can no longer offer Advanced Data Protection in the United Kingdom to new users](https://support.apple.com/en-gb/122234).

What is the end game here? If it was an ISP doing this, I don’t think I would bat an eyelid, but this is a device manufacturer who just so happens to make their own operating system. Why are they now rolling out age verification? The UK law does not compel operating systems or hardware manufacturers to verify a user’s age in the same way that it requires service providers to. Why the overreach? Why the “helpfulness” from Apple? What is this really about?

Apple’s move sets the stage for others to follow. In [my earlier rant about Apple](https://cybrkyd.com/post/apple-bricking-iphones-and-ipads-in-the-uk-to-protect-kids/), I am impatiently waiting for Android to go the same way. It will come, of that I am certain.

Is this a precursor to what Apple soon needs to do in California by 2027? Their initial move in the UK market doesn’t make any sense. I’m trying to think about this from a purely business perspective – is this their test bed for what they will soon have to do in their home town?

When the UK High Court issues a directive to block a domain, ISPs are compelled by law to block it. We have seen this before in the UK, many times. It helps to combat Internet piracy. Sure, it is like chopping off one of Hydra’s heads but it will at least put in place an inconvenience and hopefully, eventually, reduce Internet piracy.

This move by Apple, I can sort of see it in the same light. However, the key difference here is that this is a unilateral decision by Apple. In much the same way that they unilaterally opted to remove encryption for some of their iCloud data offerings. There was no High Court order; the UK law requires service providers – not operating systems – to block or unblock content. That is my understanding.

From a purely business perspective, it might be the thinking at Apple that they need to get ahead of the game. They are not entirely wrong or alone. [Systemd has had a pull request](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/24/foss_age_verification/) which adds age verification. [Flatpak is discussing the technicals](https://github.com/flatpak/xdg-desktop-portal/pull/1922) around how to implement age verification.

This is all to do with that [Californian law](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=202520260AB1043).

What I wonder about though is, from a Linux OS point of view, how would I “prove” that I’m an adult? The Systemd check seems to be configured so that only root can make changes to the data, in much the same way that the Flatpak folks are looking at. So, what does that mean? I am my own system admin… can I simply input that my one user account (that again being me) was born on `01 January 1970` and be done with it? Will my system believe me? And how about their system, whoever “they” are? If not, then what else will I need to do to prove my birth date and age? Who will check if root can’t be trusted? How will they check?

Where is all this going? Who knows? The lawmakers seem to have it all figured out. My questions have no answers. Not yet. At least, none that I could find. Non-Systemd Linux distros are laughing right now, and pointing fingers, doing that “Ha ha” of Nelson Muntz, except, they are not the bullies. They are making fun of the bullied.

summary_preview: 苹果正在英国为iPhone和iPad推出年龄验证功能，要求设备“所有者”验证年龄才能使用某些服务。此举与2025年苹果将停止向新用户提供“高级数据保护”功能同步，显示出其在英国市场逐步让步于监管压力。尽管英国法律并未强制操作系统或硬件厂商进行年龄验证，苹果却主动推进，引发质疑：这是否为过度干预用户隐私？作为设备制造商与操作系统拥有者，苹果的行为更像是在自我监管，而非响应法律要求。其背后动机耐人寻味，或意在为未来更广泛的数据控制铺路。此举或将为其他厂商树立先例，尤其是Android系统，预计不久后也将跟进类似措施，形成全球性的设备级年龄验证趋势。

--- 元数据 ---
标题：Systemd 和 Flatpak 上的年龄验证
作者：N/A
网站：Cybrkyd
最终URL：https://cybrkyd.com/post/age-verification-on-systemd-and-flatpak/
提取方式：readability
--- Markdown ---
* * *

* * *

* * *

Apple is rolling out [age verification for iPhone and iPad in the UK](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c20qwz9xzr9o). The idea is to get the device “owner” to verify their age so that they can access “certain services”. Apple has already removed encryption for UK users in 2025: [Apple can no longer offer Advanced Data Protection in the United Kingdom to new users](https://support.apple.com/en-gb/122234).

苹果正在英国推出[针对 iPhone 和 iPad 的年龄验证](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c20qwz9xzr9o)。其目的是让设备“所有者”验证自己的年龄，以便访问“某些服务”。苹果已在 2025 年为英国用户移除了加密功能：[苹果不再向英国新用户提供高级数据保护](https://support.apple.com/en-gb/122234)。

What is the end game here? If it was an ISP doing this, I don’t think I would bat an eyelid, but this is a device manufacturer who just so happens to make their own operating system. Why are they now rolling out age verification? The UK law does not compel operating systems or hardware manufacturers to verify a user’s age in the same way that it requires service providers to. Why the overreach? Why the “helpfulness” from Apple? What is this really about?

这背后的最终目的是什么？如果这是由一家互联网服务提供商（ISP）实施的，我可能不会有任何异议。但苹果是一家设备制造商，恰好还开发了自己的操作系统。为什么现在要推出年龄验证？英国法律并不要求操作系统或硬件制造商像要求服务提供商那样验证用户年龄。为什么会有这种越权行为？为什么苹果要表现出这种“热心”？这背后真正意味着什么？

Apple’s move sets the stage for others to follow. In [my earlier rant about Apple](https://cybrkyd.com/post/apple-bricking-iphones-and-ipads-in-the-uk-to-protect-kids/), I am impatiently waiting for Android to go the same way. It will come, of that I am certain.

苹果的这一举动为其他公司树立了先例。在我之前对苹果的激烈批评中（[苹果在英国通过禁用 iPhone 和 iPad 来保护儿童](https://cybrkyd.com/post/apple-bricking-iphones-and-ipads-in-the-uk-to-protect-kids/)），我正急切等待安卓系统也走上同样的道路。这一点我确信无疑。

Is this a precursor to what Apple soon needs to do in California by 2027? Their initial move in the UK market doesn’t make any sense. I’m trying to think about this from a purely business perspective – is this their test bed for what they will soon have to do in their home town?

这是否预示着苹果将在 2027 年加州必须采取的行动？他们在英国市场的初步举措似乎毫无意义。我试着从纯粹的商业角度思考——这是否是苹果在为他们自己的“家乡”即将面临的要求进行测试？

When the UK High Court issues a directive to block a domain, ISPs are compelled by law to block it. We have seen this before in the UK, many times. It helps to combat Internet piracy. Sure, it is like chopping off one of Hydra’s heads but it will at least put in place an inconvenience and hopefully, eventually, reduce Internet piracy.

当英国高等法院发布指令要求屏蔽某个域名时，互联网服务提供商必须依法执行屏蔽。我们之前在英国已经多次看到这种情况。这有助于打击网络盗版。当然，这就像斩掉九头蛇的一颗头，但至少能制造不便，最终或许能减少网络盗版。

This move by Apple, I can sort of see it in the same light. However, the key difference here is that this is a unilateral decision by Apple. In much the same way that they unilaterally opted to remove encryption for some of their iCloud data offerings. There was no High Court order; the UK law requires service providers – not operating systems – to block or unblock content. That is my understanding.

我对苹果的这一举动，也能从类似的角度理解。但关键区别在于，这是苹果单方面的决定。就像他们曾单方面决定移除部分 iCloud 数据服务的加密功能一样。没有任何高等法院的命令；英国法律要求的是服务提供商屏蔽或解封内容，而非操作系统。这是我目前的理解。

From a purely business perspective, it might be the thinking at Apple that they need to get ahead of the game. They are not entirely wrong or alone. [Systemd has had a pull request](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/24/foss_age_verification/) which adds age verification. [Flatpak is discussing the technicals](https://github.com/flatpak/xdg-desktop-portal/pull/1922) around how to implement age verification.

从纯粹的商业角度来看，苹果可能认为他们需要抢占先机。他们的想法并非完全错误，也并非孤例。[Systemd 已经提交了一个拉取请求](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/24/foss_age_verification/)，增加了年龄验证功能。[Flatpak 正在讨论技术实现细节](https://github.com/flatpak/xdg-desktop-portal/pull/1922)，探讨如何实施年龄验证。

This is all to do with that [Californian law](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=202520260AB1043).

这一切都与那项[加利福尼亚州法律](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=202520260AB1043)有关。

What I wonder about though is, from a Linux OS point of view, how would I “prove” that I’m an adult? The Systemd check seems to be configured so that only root can make changes to the data, in much the same way that the Flatpak folks are looking at. So, what does that mean? I am my own system admin… can I simply input that my one user account (that again being me) was born on `01 January 1970` and be done with it? Will my system believe me? And how about their system, whoever “they” are? If not, then what else will I need to do to prove my birth date and age? Who will check if root can’t be trusted? How will they check?

然而，我好奇的是，从 Linux 操作系统角度来看，我该如何“证明”自己已成年？Systemd 的检查似乎配置为只有 root 用户才能修改数据，这与 Flatpak 团队目前的思路类似。那么这意味着什么？我就是自己的系统管理员……我是否可以简单地输入我的唯一用户账户（也就是我本人）出生于“1970 年 1 月 1 日”，然后就完成验证了？我的系统会相信我吗？而“他们”——无论指的是谁——的系统又会如何判断？如果不行，那我还需提供哪些其他证明来证明我的出生日期和年龄？如果 root 用户不可信，谁来核实？他们又该如何核实？

Where is all this going? Who knows? The lawmakers seem to have it all figured out. My questions have no answers. Not yet. At least, none that I could find. Non-Systemd Linux distros are laughing right now, and pointing fingers, doing that “Ha ha” of Nelson Muntz, except, they are not the bullies. They are making fun of the bullied.

这一切将走向何方？谁也不知道？立法者似乎已经把一切都安排好了。我的疑问目前没有答案，至少我找不到。非 Systemd 的 Linux 发行版现在正幸灾乐祸，指指点点，做出那种奈尔森·芒茨式的“哈哈哈”嘲笑，但讽刺的是，他们不是霸凌者。他们是在嘲笑那些被欺负的人。

title: The FAA’s flight restriction for drones is an attempt to criminalize filming ICE
fulltext:
--- METADATA ---
Title: The FAA’s “Temporary” Flight Restriction for Drones is a Blatant Attempt to Criminalize Filming ICE
Author: Sophia Cope
Site: Electronic Frontier Foundation
FinalURL: https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2026/04/faas-temporary-flight-restriction-drones-blatant-attempt-criminalize-filming-ice
Extraction: readability
--- MARKDOWN ---
*Legal intern Raj Gambhir was the principal author of this post.*

The Trump administration has restricted the First Amendment right to record law enforcement by issuing an unprecedented [nationwide flight restriction](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=detail_6_4375) preventing private drone operators, including professional and citizen journalists, from flying drones within half a mile of any ICE or CBP vehicle.

In January, EFF and media organizations including *The New York Times* and *The Washington Post* responded to this blatant infringement of the First Amendment by [demanding that the FAA lift this flight restriction](https://medialaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/01.28.26faa.pdf). Over two months later, we’re still waiting for the FAA to respond to our letter.

The First Amendment guarantees the [right to record](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/02/yes-you-have-right-film-ice) law enforcement. As we have seen with the extrajudicial killings of [George Floyd](https://www.nytimes.com/video/us/100000007159353/george-floyd-arrest-death-video.html), [Renée Good](https://www.fox9.com/video/fmc-ftqi85b077jb7moh), and [Alex Pretti](https://www.nytimes.com/video/us/100000010668660/new-video-analysis-reveals-flawed-and-fatal-decisions-in-shooting-of-pretti.html), capturing law enforcement on camera can drive accountability and raise awareness of police misconduct.

### **A 21-Month Long “Temporary” Flight Restriction?**

The FAA regularly issues temporary flight restrictions (TFRs) to prevent people from flying into designated airspace. TFRs are [usually](https://www.faa.gov/uas/getting_started/temporary_flight_restrictions) [issued](https://nbaa.org/aircraft-operations/airspace/alerts/notams-and-tfrs/temporary-flight-restrictions/) during natural disasters, or to protect major sporting events and government officials like the president, and in most cases last mere hours.

Not so with the restriction numbered [FDC 6/4375, which started on January 16, 2026.](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=detail_6_4375) This TFR lasts for [21 months](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=list)—until October 29, 2027—and covers the *entire nation*. It prevents any person from flying any unmanned aircraft (i.e., a drone) within 3000 feet, measured horizontally, of any of the “facilities and mobile assets,” including “ground vehicle convoys and their associated escorts,” of the Departments of Defense, Energy, Justice, and Homeland Security. Violators can be subject to criminal and civil penalties, and risk having their drones seized or destroyed.

In practical terms, this TFR means that anyone flying their drone within a half mile of an ICE or CBP agent’s car (a DHS “mobile asset”) is liable to face criminal charges and have their drone shot down. The practical unfairness of this TFR is underscored by the fact that immigration agents often use [unmarked rental cars](https://www.wired.com/story/confessions-of-the-ice-agent-whisperer/), use cars [without license plates](https://www.npr.org/2025/10/28/nx-s1-5587653/trump-administration-relying-on-unmarked-vehicles-in-immigration-enforcement), or [switch](https://www.sj-r.com/story/news/state/2025/12/05/agency-confirms-ice-agents-are-swapping-out-license-plates-in-illinois/87610451007/) the license plates of their cars to carry out their operations. Nor do they provide prior warning of those operations.

### **The TFR is an Unconstitutional Infringement of Free Speech**

While the FAA asserts that the TFR is grounded in its lawful authority, the flight restriction not only violates multiple constitutional rights, but also the agency’s own regulations.

**First Amendment violation.** As we highlighted in the letter, nearly every federal appeals court has recognized the First Amendment right of Americans to record law enforcement officers performing their official duties. By subjecting drone operators to criminal and civil penalties, along with the potential destruction or seizure of their drone, the TFR punishes—without the required justifications—lawful recording of law enforcement officers, including immigration agents.  

**Fifth Amendment violation.** The Fifth Amendment guarantees the right to due process, which includes being given fair notice before being deprived of liberty or property by the government. Under the flight restriction, advanced notice isn’t even possible. As discussed above, drone operators can’t know whether they are within 3000 horizontal feet of unmarked DHS vehicles. Yet the TFR allows the government to capture or even shoot down a drone if it flies within the TFR radius, and to impose criminal and civil penalties on the operator.

**Violations of FAA regulations.** In issuing a TFR, the FAA’s own regulations require the agency to [“specify\[\] the hazard or condition requiring”](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/CFR-2011-title14-vol2/pdf/CFR-2011-title14-vol2-sec91-137.pdf) the restriction. Furthermore, the FAA must provide accredited news representatives with a point of contact to obtain permission to fly drones within the restricted area. The FAA has satisfied neither of these requirements in issuing its nationwide ban on drones getting near government vehicles.

### **EFF Demands Rescission of the TFR**

We don’t believe it’s a coincidence that the TFR was put in place in January 2026, at the height of the Minneapolis anti-ICE protests, shortly after the killing of Renée Good and shortly before the shooting of Alex Pretti. After both of those tragedies, civilian recordings played a vital role in contradicting the government’s false account of the events.

By punishing civilians for recording federal law enforcement officers, the TFR helps to shield ICE and other immigration agents from scrutiny and accountability. It also discourages the exercise of a key First Amendment right. EFF has long advocated for the right to record the police, and exercising that right today is more important than ever.

Finally, while recording law enforcement is protected by the First Amendment, be aware that officers may retaliate against you for exercising this right. Please refer to [our guidance](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/02/yes-you-have-right-film-ice) on safely recording law enforcement activities.
summary_preview:
美国联邦航空管理局（FAA）颁布了一项前所未有的全国性飞行禁令，禁止私人无人机在任何国土安全部（ICE）或边境巡逻队（CBP）车辆半英里范围内飞行，此举严重侵犯了公民记录执法活动的宪法第一修正案权利。这一限制实质上是对记者和普通民众拍摄执法行动的压制，尤其针对那些揭露执法不当行为的影像记录。

电子前沿基金会（EFF）联合《纽约时报》、《华盛顿邮报》等媒体机构已正式要求FAA撤销该禁令，但至今未获回应。历史经验表明，公众拍摄执法过程是推动问责的重要手段——从乔治·弗洛伊德、蕾妮·古德到亚历克斯·普雷蒂的悲剧，视频记录屡次揭示执法中的致命错误与系统性问题。

尽管法律保障公民有权记录执法行为，但执法人员仍可能对拍摄者进行报复。EFF建议公众在记录时遵守安全指南，以保障自身权益与人身安全。此次禁令不仅限制了信息自由，更构成对公民监督权的公然威胁。

--- METADATA ---
Title: The FAA’s “Temporary” Flight Restriction for Drones is a Blatant Attempt to Criminalize Filming ICE
Author: Sophia Cope
Site: Electronic Frontier Foundation
FinalURL: https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2026/04/faas-temporary-flight-restriction-drones-blatant-attempt-criminalize-filming-ice
Extraction: readability
--- MARKDOWN ---
*Legal intern Raj Gambhir was the principal author of this post.*

--- 元数据 ---
标题：美国联邦航空管理局对无人机的“临时”飞行限制是公然试图将拍摄移民与海关执法（ICE）行为刑事化
作者：索菲亚·科普
网站：电子前沿基金会
最终网址：https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2026/04/faas-temporary-flight-restriction-drones-blatant-attempt-criminalize-filming-ice
提取来源：readability
--- MARKDOWN ---
*法律实习生拉吉·甘比尔是本文的主要作者。*

The Trump administration has restricted the First Amendment right to record law enforcement by issuing an unprecedented [nationwide flight restriction](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=detail_6_4375) preventing private drone operators, including professional and citizen journalists, from flying drones within half a mile of any ICE or CBP vehicle.

特朗普政府通过发布一项前所未有的[全国性飞行限制](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=detail_6_4375)，限制了公民记录执法行为的言论自由权利，禁止私人无人机操作员（包括专业和公民记者）在任何移民与海关执法局（ICE）或边境巡逻局（CBP）车辆半英里范围内飞行无人机。

In January, EFF and media organizations including *The New York Times* and *The Washington Post* responded to this blatant infringement of the First Amendment by [demanding that the FAA lift this flight restriction](https://medialaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/01.28.26faa.pdf). Over two months later, we’re still waiting for the FAA to respond to our letter.

1月，电子前沿基金会（EFF）以及《纽约时报》和《华盛顿邮报》等媒体组织，针对这一明显侵犯第一修正案的行为，[要求联邦航空管理局（FAA）撤销该飞行限制](https://medialaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/01.28.26faa.pdf)。然而，两个月过去了，我们仍在等待FAA对我们的信函作出回应。

The First Amendment guarantees the [right to record](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/02/yes-you-have-right-film-ice) law enforcement. As we have seen with the extrajudicial killings of [George Floyd](https://www.nytimes.com/video/us/100000007159353/george-floyd-arrest-death-video.html), [Renée Good](https://www.fox9.com/video/fmc-ftqi85b077jb7moh), and [Alex Pretti](https://www.nytimes.com/video/us/100000010668660/new-video-analysis-reveals-flawed-and-fatal-decisions-in-shooting-of-pretti.html), capturing law enforcement on camera can drive accountability and raise awareness of police misconduct.

第一修正案保障了[记录执法行为的权利](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/02/yes-you-have-right-film-ice)。正如我们从[乔治·弗洛伊德](https://www.nytimes.com/video/us/100000007159353/george-floyd-arrest-death-video.html)、[蕾内·古德](https://www.fox9.com/video/fmc-ftqi85b077jb7moh)和[亚历克斯·普雷蒂](https://www.nytimes.com/video/us/100000010668660/new-video-analysis-reveals-flawed-and-fatal-decisions-in-shooting-of-pretti.html)的法外杀害事件中所看到的那样，对执法行为进行影像记录能够推动问责，提高公众对警察不当行为的认识。

### **A 21-Month Long “Temporary” Flight Restriction?**

### **长达21个月的“临时”飞行限制？**

The FAA regularly issues temporary flight restrictions (TFRs) to prevent people from flying into designated airspace. TFRs are [usually](https://www.faa.gov/uas/getting_started/temporary_flight_restrictions) [issued](https://nbaa.org/aircraft-operations/airspace/alerts/notams-and-tfrs/temporary-flight-restrictions/) during natural disasters, or to protect major sporting events and government officials like the president, and in most cases last mere hours.

联邦航空管理局（FAA）通常会发布临时飞行限制（TFR），以防止人员进入指定空域。TFR一般在自然灾害期间、大型体育赛事或保护政府高官（如总统）时发布，且大多数情况下仅持续数小时。

Not so with the restriction numbered [FDC 6/4375, which started on January 16, 2026.](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=detail_6_4375) This TFR lasts for [21 months](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=list)—until October 29, 2027—and covers the *entire nation*. It prevents any person from flying any unmanned aircraft (i.e., a drone) within 3000 feet, measured horizontally, of any of the “facilities and mobile assets,” including “ground vehicle convoys and their associated escorts,” of the Departments of Defense, Energy, Justice, and Homeland Security. Violators can be subject to criminal and civil penalties, and risk having their drones seized or destroyed.

但编号为FDC 6/4375的这一限制却不同，它始于2026年1月16日。[该TFR持续21个月](https://tfr.faa.gov/tfr3/?page=list)，直至2027年10月29日，覆盖全国范围。该限制禁止任何人将任何无人驾驶航空器（即无人机）在距国防部、能源部、司法部和国土安全部的“设施和移动资产”（包括“地面车辆车队及其随行护送车辆”）水平3000英尺范围内飞行。违规者可能面临刑事和民事处罚，其无人机也可能被没收或摧毁。

In practical terms, this TFR means that anyone flying their drone within a half mile of an ICE or CBP agent’s car (a DHS “mobile asset”) is liable to face criminal charges and have their drone shot down. The practical unfairness of this TFR is underscored by the fact that immigration agents often use [unmarked rental cars](https://www.wired.com/story/confessions-of-the-ice-agent-whisperer/), use cars [without license plates](https://www.npr.org/2025/10/28/nx-s1-5587653/trump-administration-relying-on-unmarked-vehicles-in-immigration-enforcement), or [switch](https://www.sj-r.com/story/news/state/2025/12/05/agency-confirms-ice-agents-are-swapping-out-license-plates-in-illinois/87610451007/) the license plates of their cars to carry out their operations. Nor do they provide prior warning of those operations.

实际上，这项TFR意味着，任何在移民与海关执法局（ICE）或边境巡逻局（CBP）执法人员车辆半英里内飞行无人机的人，都可能面临刑事指控，其无人机也可能被击落。这项限制的不公平性显而易见：移民执法人员常常使用[无标志的租赁车辆](https://www.wired.com/story/confessions-of-the-ice-agent-whisperer/)、[无车牌的车辆](https://www.npr.org/2025/10/28/nx-s1-5587653/trump-administration-relying-on-unmarked-vehicles-in-immigration-enforcement)或[更换车牌](https://www.sj-r.com/story/news/state/2025/12/05/agency-confirms-ice-agents-are-swapping-out-license-plates-in-illinois/87610451007/)来执行任务，且从不提前发出警告。

### **The TFR is an Unconstitutional Infringement of Free Speech**

### **该TFR构成违宪的言论自由侵犯**

While the FAA asserts that the TFR is grounded in its lawful authority, the flight restriction not only violates multiple constitutional rights, but also the agency’s own regulations.

尽管FAA声称该TFR基于其合法权力，但该飞行限制不仅违反了多项宪法权利，也违背了该机构自身的规章。

**First Amendment violation.** As we highlighted in the letter, nearly every federal appeals court has recognized the First Amendment right of Americans to record law enforcement officers performing their official duties. By subjecting drone operators to criminal and civil penalties, along with the potential destruction or seizure of their drone, the TFR punishes—without the required justifications—lawful recording of law enforcement officers, including immigration agents.

**第一修正案侵权**。正如我们在信中指出的那样，几乎所有联邦上诉法院都承认美国公民有权记录正在执行公务的执法官员。通过将无人机操作员置于刑事和民事处罚之下，并可能没收或摧毁其无人机，该TFR在缺乏必要正当理由的情况下，惩罚了对执法官员（包括移民执法人员）的合法记录行为。

**Fifth Amendment violation.** The Fifth Amendment guarantees the right to due process, which includes being given fair notice before being deprived of liberty or property by the government. Under the flight restriction, advanced notice isn’t even possible. As discussed above, drone operators can’t know whether they are within 3000 horizontal feet of unmarked DHS vehicles. Yet the TFR allows the government to capture or even shoot down a drone if it flies within the TFR radius, and to impose criminal and civil penalties on the operator.

**第五修正案侵权**。第五修正案保障了正当程序的权利，包括在政府剥夺个人自由或财产之前给予公正通知。然而，在该飞行限制下，提前通知根本不可能。如上所述，无人机操作员无法确定自己是否处于无标识国土安全部车辆3000英尺水平距离内。但TFR允许政府在无人机进入限制区域时将其捕获甚至击落，并对操作员处以刑事和民事处罚。

**Violations of FAA regulations.** In issuing a TFR, the FAA’s own regulations require the agency to [“specify\[\] the hazard or condition requiring”](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/CFR-2011-title14-vol2/pdf/CFR-2011-title14-vol2-sec91-137.pdf) the restriction. Furthermore, the FAA must provide accredited news representatives with a point of contact to obtain permission to fly drones within the restricted area. The FAA has satisfied neither of these requirements in issuing its nationwide ban on drones getting near government vehicles.

**违反FAA规章**。根据FAA自身规定，在发布TFR时，必须明确说明“引发限制的危险或情况”。此外，FAA必须为经认证的新闻代表提供联系人，以获得在受限区域内飞行无人机的许可。然而，FAA在发布这项全国性无人机禁令时，既未说明危险情况，也未提供联系人信息。

### **EFF Demands Rescission of the TFR**

### **EFF要求撤销该TFR**

We don’t believe it’s a coincidence that the TFR was put in place in January 2026, at the height of the Minneapolis anti-ICE protests, shortly after the killing of Renée Good and shortly before the shooting of Alex Pretti. After both of those tragedies, civilian recordings played a vital role in contradicting the government’s false account of the events.

我们并不认为巧合的是，该TFR于2026年1月实施，正值明尼阿波利斯反ICE抗议活动达到高潮，就在蕾内·古德遇害之后，以及亚历克斯·普雷蒂被枪杀之前。在两起悲剧事件发生后，公众拍摄的影像资料在揭露政府虚假陈述方面发挥了关键作用。

By punishing civilians for recording federal law enforcement officers, the TFR helps to shield ICE and other immigration agents from scrutiny and accountability. It also discourages the exercise of a key First Amendment right. EFF has long advocated for the right to record the police, and exercising that right today is more important than ever.

通过惩罚普通民众记录联邦执法人员的行为，该TFR帮助移民执法机构（如ICE）逃避监督和问责，也压制了第一修正案所保障的重要权利的行使。EFF长期以来一直倡导公众记录执法行为的权利，而如今，行使这一权利比以往任何时候都更加重要。

Finally, while recording law enforcement is protected by the First Amendment, be aware that officers may retaliate against you for exercising this right. Please refer to [our guidance](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/02/yes-you-have-right-film-ice) on safely recording law enforcement activities.

最后，尽管记录执法行为受第一修正案保护，但执法官员可能会对行使这一权利的人进行报复。请参考[我们的安全指南](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/02/yes-you-have-right-film-ice)，了解如何安全地记录执法活动。
